Actionable

I want to start by recognizing your work on the client account.
When the deal was at risk, you stepped in, and we closed it on time.
One development area: the dashboard underdelivered, so let us make a plan.
In my report I will say you told me you were stretched too thin.
Thanks for making time, Sofia. I want to start with recognition, because your client work this cycle has been genuinely commendable.
Thank you. That account was a hard one, so it is good to hear that.

Let me be specific. When the renewal was at risk in March, you stepped in, rebuilt the proposal overnight, and we kept the contract. That is a real accomplishment, and it is exactly the strength I want to call out.
I appreciate you naming the result, not just saying good job. So, where do I need to grow?

One development area: the quarterly dashboard underdelivered, it was two weeks late and missing the regional split. I want to make this actionable, so let us agree on a template and a check-in each Friday. That follow-through is on both of us.
That is fair. Honestly, I was stretched too thin that quarter, the client work took everything I had. A template and a Friday check-in would help.
Good, in my note to the director I will say you told me you were stretched too thin, so we can balance the load. And a review is two-way: what feedback do you have for me on how I lead the team?
Since you ask, earlier in the year I was not sure what you expected from the dashboard. Clearer goals up front would help. But this review is the clearest I have had here.
I want to start by recognizing your work, it is been commendable.
When it was at risk, you stepped in, and the result was a win.
One development area, let us make it actionable with a follow-through.
She told me she was stretched too thin, so we are rebalancing.
What feedback do you have for me on how I lead the team?

Prep note to myself, the night before I run mid-year reviews for my team at Brink's. Three conversations tomorrow, and I want to get the structure right this time, because last cycle I rambled and people left unsure of where they stood. So here is my plan. For each person, I open with genuine recognition, something specific they did well, not a vague nice job. Then I use the STAR method to keep it concrete: I name the situation, the task they owned, the action they took, and the result it produced. That stops feedback from turning into opinion. Then, and this is the part I usually rush, I name one development area honestly, framed as actionable, with a clear follow-through we agree on together. With Sofia, the strength is obvious, her client work has been commendable, but she underdelivered on the quarterly dashboard and I need to say so without crushing her. With the second analyst, the praise is the easy part and the development area is time management. And with the third, I want to raise the bar on his presentations, because he is ready for more. One more thing I keep forgetting: a review is a two-way street. I should ask each of them for feedback on how I lead, and actually listen instead of getting defensive. If I balance the praise with one honest development area, keep it specific with STAR, and leave each person with a clear next step, then tomorrow goes well. Sleep now, lead well in the morning.

Hi Victor, it's Clara, your coach, with a quick voice note before your review cycle tomorrow. I have three things for you, and they all come down to landing feedback that people can actually use. First, lead with recognition that is specific. Telling someone their work was commendable means nothing on its own. Tell them what they did and what it produced. That is the whole point of the STAR method, and you already know it: situation, task, action, result. When you anchor praise in a result, it sticks, and when you anchor a development area in a result, it stops feeling personal. Second, do not bury the one thing that has to change. If someone underdelivered, name it, but make it actionable, with a follow-through you both own. Vague feedback is unkind feedback, because the person cannot act on it. Third, and this is the one people forget, when you carry feedback up the chain to your director, you will be reporting what your team said, so watch your grammar. If Sofia tells you today, I am stretched too thin, then in the review tomorrow you report it as she said she was stretched too thin. The tense steps back, the pronoun shifts, and the time word moves: today becomes that day, will becomes would, can becomes could. Get that right and you sound precise instead of sloppy. Balance the praise, make the development area actionable, and report accurately. You have got this. Go raise the bar.
